reviews.review_text: This game is possibly the most under appreciated batman game ever made.
Great action, good story, amazing Xmas vibes, cool Batman. Insanely underrated.
Absolutely underrated, doesn't deserve the hate it gets as "different" from the other three Arkhams.
is it worse than the other arkham games? yes. but is it a bad game? absolutely not. infact batman is sexy enough for this game to be an 8/10 at the very least

This game was really good for a game released in 2013.I really liked the combat because it was so smooth, the story also hit different and I liked the aspect of batman becoming batman and the angry side of him, I also loved how the story explained batman and jokers early relationship.

Experience the origins of the Batman from the Arkham games! 

Without spoiling the story, I'll say it's very engaging just like the other arkham games!
The combat system is just as fantastic as it ever was!
The gadgets truly make you feel like Batman!
Explore areas of Gotham just like the Dark Knight!
Loads of Unlockable and side quests to do, truly great stuff!
10/10 game for a 10/10 super hero! Definitely worth playing!

Batman: Arkham Origins is a seamless prequel that fits perfectly into the series’ winning formula. It sticks closely to what made the previous games great, feeling less like a departure and more like another strong chapter in one cohesive story. The standout here is its focus on lesser-known villains like Black Mask and Anarky, while still bringing in heavy hitters like Joker, Bane, and Penguin to anchor the narrative. Exploring a cleaner, pre-Arkham City version of Gotham is a nice touch, and the story does a great job showing Batman in his earlier, more inexperienced days - especially his first encounters with key characters. It may not reinvent the formula, but it doesn’t need to. Like the rest of the series, it’s a polished, highly enjoyable experience that stands shoulder to shoulder with the others.

Batman: Arkham Origins is seriously underrated.

Even though it wasn’t made by the main Arkham team, it still delivers an amazing Batman experience. The darker Christmas setting gives Gotham a unique atmosphere, and the story shows a younger, angrier Batman learning what it means to become the Dark Knight.

The boss fights are easily some of the best in the entire Arkham series, especially Deathstroke. Combat and stealth still feel great, and exploring Gotham during the snowfall looks incredible.

People compare it too much to Arkham City, but Origins deserves credit for its story, soundtrack, and character development. It made Batman feel more human while still making you feel powerful.

Underrated gem in the Arkham series.

I love the gameplay, the controls are pretty similar to other Arkham games so it was pretty easy to get into. I've had to watch walkthroughs a few times for the game, as it's a bit more difficult puzzle-wise. But as someone who loves all things Batman-related this is a double thumbs up and 10 stars from me.

I am pretty surprised this game gets a lot of hate. I highly recommend a play if its one you haven't played.

However the bugs are ridiculous, especially for certain unlockables and challenges (I had to pop some achievements manually as the game kept not triggering the unlock conditions).

Coming to origins are playing city and asylum, its a bit less smoother than those 2 but still captures the spirit and atmosphere very well. Overall still a very good arkham game
The weakest Arkham series game, but still a great game. Does not have that many city details as Arkham trilogy.

Arkham City 1.5, the snow DLC. Was honestly super close to dropping the game in the first 1/3 but then the buzzword villains name came up so I had to keep going and now I'm glad I did. Arkham Origins is a leap above Asylum but is not on the level of city. If you took the open world of City and made it baron and boring to explore you would have Origins. And the empty lacklustre open world isn't the main issue, the constant stuttering and frame drops drive me mental. It's almost exclusive to the free roam so you want to try and get to your location ASAP to save the slideshow and it's disappointed to explore because of that. Other than a weak open world and technical issues the fundamental combat, gadgets and stealth all feel on par with City so it's solid as ever and a fun time to breeze through.

The story as said previously really did nothing for me in the first 1/3, the Penguin is a cool guy but he didn't really do anything to hold my attention, then the main villain rocks up and the story really just comes to life and the cutscenes, environments and set pieces also go up in quality. For a pre-sequel it didn't change enough, and if anything took some away while adding issues, but the foundations that it stands on are already so solid that you still just enjoy playing it for it's pretty short run time. Also what goober designed the top left of the map in such a way that you had to do a big detour around Shelton Park just to get to Park Row or Bowery that was just so annoying.
short but sweet, very short story, but thrillful combat and gameplay

A very solid entry in an already very solid series. While Arkham Origins doesn't do a whole lot to shake up the gameplay from Arkham Asylum and Arkham City (in fact it's basically the same exact gameplay as Arkham City), the combat is still very fun, and the story is all very engaging and has a lot to say about Batman and the Joker. Definitely not the best game in the series, but it's still a great and well-made experience that I very much enjoy. I'll give it an 8/10

[Edit: story got better but, after Deadshot somehow being able to still shoot the hostage during the rendered cutscene, I decided to give up on the game. Good lord, the bugs. Get it on a big sale if you have the patience that I've apparently run out of. lol]

After seeing many reviews, I let this game sit in the library for a long time to put some space between it and the other Arkham games so as to soften my expectations. I'm glad I did, but the negative reviews are still justified and I find myself slogging through the game just to finish the story.


Pros:

+ Decent story so far, if predictable.

+ Music is great. Love the 'Carol of the Bells' mesh for Xmas. 


Cons: 

- Combat feels half-azzed & clunky compared to the other games in the series, to the point of frustrating when you're trying to work up the achievement lines.

- The Challenge Paths & gadgets locked behind them. Who in the murple testes designed this system? The challenges have to be done in order and you have limited instances in which to achieve some of them (with certain Predator instances not having all of the options to complete said challenges...really? It doesn't count because I used explosive gel on a wood panel instead of a concrete wall? It still fuggin detonated right in front of their eyeballs!). My generation is too GD busy and the younger generations don't have the attention span for something this pedantic anymore. If the team did this to encourage replayability...yeah, no. Opposite effect for me. 

- Enemies getting stuck in walls or corners. Annoying during Pred encounters. Absolutely stupid when you have to interrogate them and can't get "close" enough for the option to pop. You either fly away & come back or just restart when that doesn't work. 

I'm not even halfway done with this game and, unlike the others in the series, there's no way I'm sticking around to finish the Riddler challenges. Get this on a super-discounted sale and just knockout the story and side-story quests, IMO.

The dark palette of the environment, Gotham at Christmas, and the open world are real added values to the game. 

The fight system is really interesting; it is easy to learn but hard to master, without falling into the trap of being impossible just because there are too many combos to learn. 

Overall, it is a really cool game where the core of the Batman franchise is respected: dark, gritty, and story-driven. I strongly recommend it.

Batman: Arkham Origins is an exciting action game with a dark and immersive atmosphere. The story shows a younger and more aggressive Batman facing dangerous assassins on Christmas Eve in Gotham City. The combat system is fun and smooth, and the boss fights are some of the best in the Arkham series. Even though it is less innovative than previous Arkham games, it is still a great experience for Batman fans.

I had never played Arkham Origins until recently. Over the years I had only thought of this game as some mediocre cashgrab spin off that wasn't even developed by Rocksteady.

So imagine my surprise when I finally played and discovered that this is an incredibly solid game.

Gameplay wise, it is pretty much just Arkham City with some new gadgets, which is not a complaint whatsover given that Arkham City has solid gameplay already. The reuse of the Arkham City map for Old Gotham is a clever use of old assets. Since this game takes place roughly a decade before Arkham Asylum, it is quite a treat to see the section of Gotham that eventually is turned into Arkham City. 

Where this game really shines is the story. In the story, we get to see the foundation of Batman's relationships with his allies and enemies. We get see Batman build the myth that strikes fear into the hearts of criminals.

I would absolutely recommend playing it for yourself.

The Best Boss Fights in the Series
​If there is one area where Origins undeniably surpasses both Asylum and City, it is the boss encounters. WB Montréal understood how to test the player's mastery of the game's mechanics.
​Deathstroke: The cinematic, parry-heavy showdown with Deathstroke remains a high-water mark for superhero gaming combat. It demands perfect timing and reflexes.
​Bane: Instead of reducing Bane to a mindless, hulking brute, Origins presents him as the terrifying, hyper-intelligent tactician he is in the comics, culminating in a genuinely nerve-wracking predator sequence.
A great edition to the Arkham series that doesn't get enough love. Although outshined by it's other games this one tells a completely different story and is a necessity to play.

underrated arkham game, great story and beginning to the batman journey.

As a gameplay fix between City and Knight, Origins does well enough.  You get most of what you like from the earlier titles: free flow combat, stealth takedowns, mostly iconic adversaries (and one or two jobbers), collecting activities, and an expanding range of gadgets that provide metroidvania-like access to collectables and other areas.  The free-flow combat is especially engaging, and enemies are no longer designated to their weapons.  Expect any random thug to pick up a dropped gun, knife, or shield and keep that device in play until everyone is defeated.  This annoyed me at first, but once you wrap your head around the more dynamic free-flow loop and intercept enemies before they re-arm, it can be quite fun.

Conspicuously absent are the visual Riddler riddles.  There are a few other visual items to scan, but because the Riddler of this game is an earlier incarnation of the character, he doesn't send you taunting riddles or set up death traps for Batman to escape.  In place of the Riddler trophies, you now have hidden extortion data which must be collected in sets of 10 to unlock audio reels, which used to be their own collectable in previous games.

The overall city map is now larger, and fast-travel points are set up to get you around quickly.  But the map does suffer from a few notable 'dry' areas, especially around the Gotham bridge.  Arkham City was more right-sized, with collectables and activities that kept he player more engaged.  The player should never be more than a few grapple boosts and glides away from their next goal.

My biggest gripe about this game is that many of the enemies are absolute punch-sponges or long sequences of rapid-tap events.  Bane is notably frustrating to fight because he's invulnerable while powered up on Venom, and has limited windows for engagement.  Firefly is equally tiresome, as much of his fight is spent waiting for the next rapid-tap cue.  At the opposite end of things: some of the bosses are barely bosses, little more than a touch of story engagement.

I finished this in mid-2026.   Many of the game's achievements are no longer available since the multi-player portion of the game has gone offline.  Some seem to require the DLC, even though they are listed with the base game.

Overall C+/B- game.  Buy it as a complete collection on one of the regular sales for the best game-to-dollar benefit.

This game is old, but I still feel the need to rate it thumbs down. It's really bad compared to the rest of the Arkham games. It's very poorly optimized, everything is grey and foggy, the city layout isn't good, and combat isn't as polished or fun as other games. I wonder if we didn't get to see the Bat Cave in Arkham Knight because it appeared in this.

As someone doing a play-through of all arkham games, I can see why people were not as thrilled to play this compared to the previous or later entry. Arkham Asylum was the original idea, Arkham City was the idea taken to the power of 10. Arkham Origins is... Weird.

The very first cue that something's odd with this game is in the name itself. "Arkham -ORIGINS-". That being said it actually has NOTHING to do with amadeus arkham or the arkham asylum. It just takes place in Arkham City before it was Arkham City and when it was just Gotham, with the addition of a new part of the map on the south side. Not to mention that while Arkham City felt like the map was forcefully detached from the rest of Gotham because it was built that way, having an entire section of Gotham as an island is.... It just doesn't feel right. It's like if the entirety of long island had no connection to the rest of new york. The entire map just feels odd if you consider it being part of gotham as a whole.

The story also makes very little sense. Basically: "Black Mask escapes prison and hired a bunch of killers to kill you; defeat the hired hitmen and black mask". Plot twist aside the plot feels very much "villain of the week" from a weekly show.

The game also feels... Cheaper? than Arkham City. All riddler's encounters are nothing but a still image compared to the previous animations, there were weird bugs, including having a flickering/invisible killer croc fight in the prologue. This comes after having Arkham City with basically 0 bugs. The UI is a MESS and much harder to understand than the previous game, there's no mouse navigation (another thing the previous game had), and the subtle mechanic to have the game "guess" where you want to grapple using your camera feels wonkier than ever before. It was damn near perfect in City and Asylum also did a good job, but here it seems that it assumes you want to grapple into the nearest thing instead of the thing closest to the center of the screen.

The boss fights are a joke. There's either the comically annoying and hard bane fighters or the "scripted" fights where everything is on rails. This includes the executioner (minor spoiler: He dies in one hit, the entire fight is a fakeout) and Slade and firefly fights being nothing more than an interactive cutscene.

Voice actors have changed, most notably for batman. Roger Smith isn't "bad", but we all had gotten used to Kevin Conroy and having him for the previous game made the change more noticeable. Every time batman opens his mouth to speak it just feels wrong without him. Other minor nitpicks include changing the way the joker looks to the point that it looks like a different character to the previous games, and changing the way he first met with harley (as revealed in the Arkham Asylum collectible tapes).

I will have to admit, being able to actually be in the batcave for once is one of the biggest pro's that this game gets and it's a well deserving one, together with a more noticeable and interesting background OST and a much improved "investigation mode" for crimes, including being able to rewind or fast forward a time line of events, but nothing will make me think this game couldn't be slightly worse and be called a free mod for Arkham City, or a DLC for it. Instead WB is more than happy to ask money for DLC that are extra mission challenges and skins for characters, when the entire online section of this game was shut down a decade ago, merely 3 years after the game came out. I may "not recommend this game" for the sake of making my dislike towards the decision to release this instead of making a more polished game after Arkham Knights clear, but I don't think it's a "BAD" game, I just think it had some big shoes to fill and it was rushed to fill them so WB could make money instead of waiting for the proper sequel.

Really wanted black mask to be the main villain instead of Joker, it would have been a nice change but whatever I guess. I like what they did with Bane in this game and the Dynamite between Bruce and Alfred was definitely Oscar worthy. But the gameplay feels like ♥♥♥♥ I swear there's so many bugs in this game that I just can't stand.
